name: Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy (25-OH-D)
summary: Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D is the main circulating form of vitamin D and the standard indicator of overall vitamin D status. It reflects intake from sun exposure, diet, and supplements over the preceding weeks.
whatItMeans: 25-OH-D is the storage form the body builds up before converting it to the active hormone, so it is the best single marker of vitamin D stores. The NIH Food and Nutrition Board considers levels of 20 ng/mL (50 nmol/L) or more adequate for bone and general health for most people, levels below 12 ng/mL at risk of deficiency, and the older Endocrine Society framework labeled <20 ng/mL deficient, 20–29 ng/mL insufficient, and ≥30 ng/mL sufficient. Both very low and very high values can be unfavorable, so this marker is interpreted in context. This is educational information, not a diagnosis.
raises: Dietary sources (fatty fish, fortified milk, egg yolk)
Lower body fat (less sequestration of vitamin D in adipose tissue)
lowers: Limited sun exposure (indoor lifestyle, sunscreen, high latitude, winter)
Malabsorption (celiac, Crohn's, bariatric surgery) and fat-malabsorption states
Chronic kidney or liver disease impairing hydroxylation
confounders: Assays differ in how they measure D2 vs D3, causing between-lab variability
Recent high-dose supplementation can transiently raise levels
Marked seasonal variation (higher in late summer, lower in late winter)
Total 25-OH-D may not reflect bioavailable vitamin D in conditions altering vitamin D-binding protein (e.g. pregnancy)

ranges.note: NIH/FNB: ≥20 ng/mL (50 nmol/L) adequate for most; <12 ng/mL at risk of deficiency. Older Endocrine Society framework: <20 deficient, 20–29 insufficient, ≥30 sufficient; >150 ng/mL risks toxicity. Upper bound shown reflects a commonly used adequacy ceiling.

references.title: Vitamin D - Health Professional Fact Sheet | NIH Office of Dietary Supplements
